frameworks.name: Mean Reversion Money Maker (MR signal)
frameworks.description: His core timing signal: a daily mean-reversion backtest he claims shows a 100% win rate. When assets are 'really really really hot' (historic mean-reversion spikes), the statistically highest-probability outcome is a pullback or sideways chop — so he sells covered calls / hedges into strength (used on ALAB into earnings and on AMD after its historic spike) and trusts price will 'come down to earth'.

frameworks.name: IA13
frameworks.description: His basket of 13 AI names (includes AMD, ALAB, MRVL, MU, GOOG, TSLA etc). Game plan: hold until 2028-2030 because 'they are going to make huge money', which is why he buys it with massive leverage (LEAPS/synthetic longs). Newcomers are told to pick only the 3-5 highest-conviction names from the basket, not all 13. He may rotate from IA13 into SOL if crypto regulatory clarity (Clarity Act) lands.

frameworks.name: IADSS (with Optimized Trend and Trend Model)
frameworks.description: His data-science signal suite, works on all timeframes. The Optimized Trend (part of IADSS and PTOS) is an adaptive model that automatically determines the best trend settings per asset (volatility, historical behavior) — used to read macro direction and filter bias (e.g. TSLA OT 'blue since April 16th'). The Trend Model is user-controlled/tunable with backtesting, better for trading and timing entries. They work together: OT sets macro direction, Trend fine-tunes execution and avoids poor entries. He also uses ATR optimized for 12hr and 24hr timeframes (works on 6hr too).

frameworks.name: LILO levels
frameworks.description: An extension gauge: the higher the LILO level, the more extended the stock. Elevated LILO zones (e.g. ALAB and AMD in May 2026) make hedging/covered calls more attractive.

frameworks.name: Synthetic Longs
frameworks.description: His 'double-edged sword' leverage game: with a tiny amount of cash and margin you control millions in stock; +10-20% stock moves make big money fast but adverse moves can wipe you out, hence the need for a ~90% win rate. At times options pricing is irrational and you can enter synthetic longs that pay you to enter (infinite ROI if the stock moves right). 'It is the game IA has been playing for a very long time' — only shared after he made lifetime money.

frameworks.name: LEAPS + put-financing / covered-call playbook
frameworks.description: Buys long-dated LEAPS on high-conviction AI names and sells puts against them to slash net cost (AMD Dec 2028 $200C for net $14). Then manages winners with covered calls: sell calls into extreme strength to lock gains and raise cash; if the stock blows past the strike, buy back the call and roll up to a higher strike, possibly further out in time. 'You rarely lose with a covered call if the price goes up' — you just cap part of the gain.

frameworks.name: DO NOT BUY THE TOP entry system (IA13 catch-up plan)
frameworks.description: For late entrants: decide total AI allocation (e.g. $50k of a $250k portfolio = 20%), split into 4 equal tranches, deploy over 3-6 months either by steady DCA (one tranche every 4-6 weeks regardless of price, using the DCAS tool across assets and waiting for it to fire) or aggressively on any 10-20% pullback. Set price alerts instead of staring at charts; start with half a tranche on names already up massively.

frameworks.name: DCAS
frameworks.description: His DCA signal product (~$19): use it across different assets and 'wait for it to fire' to time tranche deployment instead of buying blind — 'it will save you millions'.

frameworks.name: Retire On Model
frameworks.description: His retirement-planning product, increased from 8 to 10 assets per plan by member request — but paired with the Conviction Rule: use fewer assets, not more, because roughly the top 0.3% of winners produce most market gains and every extra asset waters down returns. Shows exactly what you need, when, and how to spend it.

frameworks.name: Crypto Compendium & SCP Profiler

macro.view: BTC/Gold ratio (~17.84 oz, same as 2020) has all the hallmarks of a major cycle low: repeating historical pattern, halving cycle timing, clear support + reversal candle, gold potentially overextended. This is the exact opposite of the dot-com bust (echoing Cathie Wood): valuations dramatically below 1999-2000 mania while capabilities advance exponentially and costs (compute, energy, data, intelligence) collapse. 'Comically undervalued'; a new economic epoch, while bears like Michael Burry fight the last war. He lived/traded through 2000 (shorted EMC at the top).

Per Goldman Sachs: agentic AI is about to flip AI economics — agents use far more compute (think longer, check work, run 24/7), driving usage growth that turns AI from money pit into profit engine starting 2026. 'We are still super early in AI. The best part of this story is only just beginning.' The AI capex boom and hyperscaler spending cycle are in early-to-mid innings.

macro.theme: Capex eating free cash flow (the bear case)
macro.view: The 'capex boom eats all FCF' story is real and bears are using it aggressively: GOOG at 133x FCF (vs ~20x pre-COVID), planning $175-185B 2026 capex vs $59B 2025 cloud revenue, AI Overviews cannibalizing ads. Counter: the FCF drop is growth spending, not lost profitability, and cloud is a real second engine. Both sides have a point — almost no margin of safety; expect the same FUD to hit META, AMZN, MSFT and even TSLA.

MSTR holds a record 818,334 BTC (~$64B). Saylor now openly discusses small strategic BTC sales to pay STRC preferred dividends (the 'BTC breakeven ARR' math: ~2%+ annual BTC growth lets them sell tiny bits and still grow holdings). The real tension is tax: ROC treatment of STRC dividends depends on zero E&P, which BTC sales at a profit would threaten. Not desperation — 'maturing the model', though some of the market may not get it.

macro.theme: Solana as the AI-agent money layer
macro.view: Solana just became the 'credit card' for AI agents: pay.sh (Solana + Google Cloud) lets agents pay-per-use for APIs with Solana stablecoins, no accounts/subscriptions; x402 did 1M transactions in two weeks. Solana's near-zero fees make it the default money layer of the agentic internet — the missing piece for the agent economy to explode. Because of agentic AI, crypto is more required than ever: 'Enter the SOL thesis.' He may roll from IA13 into SOL if the Clarity Act passes (stablecoins + tokenization huge).

Network revenue share flipped: ETH from ~67% (late 2022) to 18% by Q1 2026; Solana leads at 27% (from 1%), Tron 25% (90%+ from USDT transfers in Asia), BNB 13%, others tiny or non-existent. Winners take most; SOL trades at only 17% of ETH market cap despite leading revenue.

Hormuz threatening to reopen knocked oil from 103 to 88 (Iran ~3-4M bpd potential supply). Lower oil → lower inflation expectations → lower Treasury yields. Whenever the 10Y threatens to break the 4.40% cap of this cycle, a 'deal leak' conveniently appears to sell the yield back down.

Robotics datasets exploded to #1 on Hugging Face in 2025 — more training data than text or image AI. Robotics is no longer 'the future'; the data is here, and the next giant AI leap will be robots doing real-world work, with Tesla, Figure, Google DeepMind all-in. 'Buckle in - HUMANOIDS ARE HERE.'

Unsupervised robotaxi fleet ramping and accelerating; Austin dominates with 27 unsupervised vehicles (71% of all unsupervised, ~70% of Austin fleet). Small base, but 'slowly then suddenly is incoming.'

Rotation into TSLA arrived as called: China wholesale +36% YoY (79,478), FSD expanding (Colombia, China incoming), key patents, WattEV $100M order for 370 Semis, a $1.7BN block buy. Musk was likely sandbagging on Optimus/Cybercab on the earnings call. 'The re-rating will be violent once the market realizes TSLA is the only scaled physical AI + consumer platform' — and it generates profits while all the others lose. TSLA/SpaceX the candidate first $100T company; Musk ~45-50% (Kalshi) to win the OpenAI case. Zero doubt on $500 soon, $1,000 eventually.

xAI merged into SpaceX → SpaceXAI, a $1.25T 'rocket + AI beast' with the end game of data centers in space. Elon leased the idle Colossus 1 supercluster (220k+ GPUs) to Anthropic — removing his rival's compute bottleneck so Claude can eat OpenAI's lunch, generating revenue and IPO fuel, plus inside intel via the Cursor stake (SpaceX owns a big stake + buyout option). Separately, Anthropic committed $200BN to Google over 5 years — 'Google probably becomes largest company on Earth tomorrow.'

AI will rip through the world faster than any prior technology; record corporate profits while ordinary people fight for scraps (Samsung workers already protesting). Expect protests, strikes, political chaos and 'AI IS BAD' campaigns. AI will make even fewer winners take even more — be forewarned and build defensive positions early; 'the people who protect themselves now won't be the ones begging for bonuses in the street later.'

He called early Q1 2026 to be 'shit' and it was; now with AI stocks rocketing like never before, 'realistically this cannot continue. Prepare for a pullback. Maybe August or sooner.' Sell-in-May narrative rarely works but Aug/Sep are the months to watch; would not chase anything here.
In the age of AGI, scarcity is needed — BTC is what you hold 'when other things turn to shit.' Once BTC clears 82k it is lift off; MSTR $300 an easy get above $120k BTC.

Blue states (CA, NY, NJ; 10.9-13.3% top rates plus heavy regulation) are bleeding trillions to 0%-tax red states (TX, FL, TN, NV). Instead of cutting taxes/regs they're raising them, accelerating the outflow — a vicious cycle of vanishing tax base and industry, leaving awful deficits.

frameworks.name: The Weekly Edge / #TAlpha

It is his system for sequencing entries: stay patient, buy dips with confirmation, trim Level 8-9+ names, set breakout alerts; Monday fireside chats highlight next setups.

frameworks.name: The Mattress

frameworks.name: BTC ETF Flow Impact Model

frameworks.name: 200 DMA Regime Filter
frameworks.description: His most important long-term trend filter for Bitcoin (and stocks like TSLA): above the 200 DMA = bullish regime where the line is dynamic support and pullbacks to it are bought aggressively; below = bearish regime where the line is dynamic resistance and rallies into it are sold. Drives whether he buys dips or sells rallies. Currently watching BTC fight $82,500 at the 200 DMA.

frameworks.name: SATS per Share / BPS (MSTR)
frameworks.description: His MSTR valuation metric (claims he coined 'SATS PER SHARE since day 1'): Bitcoin-per-Share = EPS on the Bitcoin standard. He holds MSTR because Saylor's objective is to double BPS, which compounds BTC yield per share; his own SATS/share is up 11x since first buying.

frameworks.name: 5-Layer Cake (AI stack allocation)

$600BN in annual credit-card interest payments is a record-high wealth transfer from regular Americans to the financial sector and a real headwind for household finances. With Jerome Powell gone, rates will come down and that number can fall.

frameworks.name: LEAPS + Conversion strategy
frameworks.description: Core position-building system: buy LEAPS (long-dated calls) only on companies he wants to own for years; buy more aggressively the lower the stock falls ('the lower they go the harder I go'); ALWAYS convert (exercise) into shares unless the thesis changes, because exercising triggers no capital-gains tax — you buy the stock cheap and avoid 'tax drag.' He plans cash management around the conversion bills (strike x contracts).

frameworks.name: Covered calls as cash-raising engine
frameworks.description: When he needs cash (e.g. for LEAPS conversions or tax bills) he sells covered calls 'like crazy' on core holdings (GOOG, AMD, ALAB, STM), accepting shares getting called away at strikes he's comfortable with, and buys the calls back ('covers the hedge') when he wants to restore upside — e.g. covered half the AMD hedge anticipating further upside.

frameworks.name: TA Weekly Edge (weekly TA recap)
frameworks.description: Weekly per-asset technical rundown combining ATR Levels and HR Levels (numbered buy/support zones, e.g. PLTR ATR Level 4 ~$129, AMD ATR Level 5, MU HR Level 5 ~$625), 4H mean-reversion (MR) readings (+2 = tag of a top; flattening = constructive consolidation), gaps (GOOGL $350, AMD $350), moving averages (50-day, 200-day), and whole-number resistance. Decision output is explicit: 'layering area,' 'not a buy zone,' 'not chasing,' 'monitor for stacking.'

frameworks.name: ATR model for Bitcoin regime
frameworks.description: His ATR-based model classifies BTC market regime; in this period it 'still shows bear-market conditions,' which keeps him from buying BTC at current levels and has him waiting for a pullback toward $75K (with $70K wicks) to stack.

frameworks.name: IA13
frameworks.description: His curated list of 13 AI-supercycle names (includes ALAB, AVGO, MRVL among others); used to explain why the photonics/AI interconnect bottleneck thesis has driven outperformance in these holdings, and as a screen for which names make the cut vs. weaker peers (AAOI financials not solid, LITE diluting/overvalued).

frameworks.name: IA Retire on Robotaxi Fleet Model
frameworks.description: Spreadsheet model of owning a small Tesla robotaxi fleet to retire on: goal of $10K net/month, fleet economics tables, $1.50/mile base assumption (with peak/surge pricing upside for retail owners when Tesla needs supply most), and a tax structure layer — run it through an LLC/C-Corp/S-Corp, write everything off, pay family members salaries to reduce taxable profit to zero.

frameworks.name: Tesla Profit Continuum
frameworks.description: A timeline he created mapping when each new Tesla line of business (Megapack already underway, robotaxi, Semi, etc.) starts producing impact-making revenue, with $ signs marking his estimates — used to frame Tesla as a sequence of staged profit pivots rather than a single car business.

frameworks.name: STRC Tracker
frameworks.description: A tracker he runs on Strategy's (MSTR) STRC issuance to predict when/how much BTC Strategy will buy; validated when MSTR bought ~25,000 BTC (~$2BN, $1.95BN from STRC) — 'STRC Tracker was correct.'

frameworks.name: DXY inverted 100-day lead indicator for BTC
frameworks.description: Chart framework that inverts the Dollar Index and shifts it forward 100 days against Bitcoin: a strong dollar usually pushes BTC down a few months later, so the current dollar rally signals possible near-term pressure on Bitcoin despite both rallying together now.

frameworks.name: Rule of 40 / Rule of 80 fundamentals screen

frameworks.name: Expected Value (EV) scenario analysis
frameworks.description: Takes third-party scenario probabilities (here FM Intelligence's Clarity Act analysis for BTC) and computes his own probability-weighted Expected Value, Expected Low, and Expected High. If even the conservative (low) scenario shows meaningful upside vs spot, he treats the setup as asymmetric and expresses it via long-dated options (IBIT LEAPs 1yr+).

frameworks.name: IA Trade Alert + 'tiny flutter' speculative sizing
frameworks.description: Real-time trade alerts disclosing his personal fills in full transparency (with heavy disclaimer). For speculative themes (quantum), he sizes a 'start position' so small it can go to zero ('super risky, tiny position'), picks the best risk/reward name in the theme via fundamental screening (real revenue, shipping products, balance sheet, tech moat), and treats the moonshot element as a lottery ticket on top of a real business.

frameworks.name: Structural demand-bucket valuation (SOL model)
frameworks.description: Values a crypto asset by summing concrete demand buckets that must hold/lock the token for the system to function — RWAs $525B, stablecoin settlement $150B, AI agents $27B, consumer/launchpad/DePIN $50B = ~$752B — then divides against circulating supply (~578M SOL) to justify a price target ($1,000) over a ~5-year maturation.

frameworks.name: Jevons Paradox lens on AI
frameworks.description: 'Textbook Jevons: Token price tanks, demand goes thru the roof.' Falling cost of intelligence drives insatiable demand — his rationale for staying long compute/AI-infrastructure winners even as model prices collapse, and for 'he or she who has the most compute wins'.

frameworks.name: Debasement / hard-assets thesis
frameworks.description: Central-bank money supply expansion is the primary driver of nominal wealth (millionaire explosion); the measuring stick (fiat) is shrinking. Decision rule derived: 'Why I always say be in hard assets' — real estate, stocks, crypto, scarce assets.

frameworks.name: Weekly technical recap (200-day MA regime + buy zones)
frameworks.description: Weekly chart review (delivered with Jacob) that maps every core holding to support/resistance, buy zones, breakout levels and volume confirmation. Key regime rule: the 200-day MA itself must be rising — not merely price above it — to confirm a real bull market; mean-reversion entries at the 50-day; 'line in the sand' invalidation levels (e.g. BTC $65k).

frameworks.name: Policy-following heuristic
frameworks.description: Front-run government capital: 'Everything he [Trump] backs a sector eg INTEL it booms.' When the administration announced $2B into quantum with equity stakes, he immediately screened the recipients and bought the best risk/reward name (INFQ) the same day.

frameworks.name: Contrarian sentiment signal
frameworks.description: Record-low University of Michigan consumer sentiment (lowest since 1952) is read as a contrary — often bullish — signal for stocks: 'we are at rock bottom.' Sentiment extremes are treated as inflection indicators rather than reasons to de-risk.

rules.rule: Rule du Jour #108: Discipline Beats Intelligence — self-control, not IQ or starting capital, determines long-term financial success.
rules.context: Backed by the 45-year Dunedin study of 1,037 people; 'The ultimate edge in investing is the ability to consistently choose what you want in ten years over what you want right now.'

rules.rule: Default answer to buying anything is NO — he must prove to himself why it is good after deep research, ignoring the herd.
rules.context: His personal application of the self-control rule to position initiation.

rules.rule: Never FOMO — force yourself to wait for a pullback to a high-probability support level before buying.
rules.context: 'eg took me nearly 10 mths to build my IA13 bag.'

rules.rule: Stop trying to be smart — you don't need a genius strategy, just the discipline to stick to a boring one. 'Find your rhythm.'
rules.context: Anti-cleverness principle within Rule #108.

rules.rule: Ignore the impulse: the urge to sell during a panic, chase a top, or buy the shiny new stock/crypto is the enemy of compounding.
rules.context: Compounding-protection rule; patience is 'a muscle' to be trained, not a personality type.

rules.rule: Speculative theme plays get only a tiny 'flutter' start position sized so it can go to zero.
rules.context: INFQ quantum entry: 'Super risky, tiny position - could go to zero... but let me explain why.'

rules.rule: Within a speculative theme, pick the name with real revenue and shipping products over pure story stocks — it lowers desperate-dilution risk while keeping the lottery-ticket upside.
rules.context: Why he chose INFQ ($9.5M Q1 revenue, $40M FY guide, govt/defense sensor sales) over RGTI/QBTS.

rules.rule: Follow government money: when the administration backs a sector with capital, that sector booms (e.g. Intel, now quantum).
rules.context: Triggered the same-day INFQ entry after the $2B quantum funding news.

rules.rule: He or she who has the most compute wins.
rules.context: AI-era investing heuristic, stated after detailing OpenAI's Stargate stall and the power/grid bottleneck.

rules.rule: Record-low consumer sentiment is often a contrary (and sometimes bullish) signal for stocks — rock bottom sentiment means upside.
rules.context: On the lowest University of Michigan reading since 1952.

rules.rule: Always be in hard assets — fiat debasement inflates scarce assets and erodes cash.
rules.context: 'Why i always say be in hard assets' — drawn from the global millionaire-explosion data.

rules.rule: If even the conservative (expected-low) scenario shows meaningful upside vs spot, the asymmetry justifies long-dated options (1yr+ LEAPs).
rules.context: BTC EV analysis → IBIT LEAP case.

rules.rule: The 200-day moving average itself must be rising — not just price trading above it — to confirm a real bull market.
rules.context: Applied to SOL in the weekly technical recap; also BTC must reclaim its 200-day for bulls to regain control.

rules.rule: After a violent breakout move (+40% week), expect and wait for a normal pullback rather than chasing.
rules.context: ALAB guidance in the weekly recap.

macro.theme: SpaceX/xAI IPO thesis upgraded by the S-1
macro.view: The S-1 'changed everything': 2025 core revenue $18.67B (above ~$15-16B private estimates) with real profitability; Anthropic AI compute deal of $1.25B/month (~$15B/yr) through May 2029 on Colossus systems (he thought it was $8B) proves capex monetization; 18,712 BTC treasury larger than expected; $28.5T framed TAM ($26.5T tied to AI). 'Before the S-1... you were mostly buying the story'; now it's audited-style numbers. Cursor and xAI reaching parity with OpenAI/Claude for code but much cheaper 'triggered' him further.

macro.theme: Starship V3 success into the IPO window
macro.view: Starship V3 Flight 12 nailed every major goal (deploy, re-entry, controlled splashdown) with the SpaceX IPO just 20 days away — 'perfect timing... Momentum is clearly building!'

macro.theme: AI cost crisis / end of the subsidy era
macro.view: Microsoft quietly canceled its Claude Code licenses (token billing untenable); Uber burned its 2026 AI budget in 4 months; enterprise AI prices +20-37%; GitHub dropping flat-rate plans. Opus 4.7 Max leads benchmarks at 64.8% but $11.02/task vs Composer 2.5 at 63.2% for $0.55 (20x cheaper). Cursor + cheap high-performance models (incl. xAI) win; 'Anthropic's premium pricing strategy is hitting a wall' — either enterprises slash usage or labs absorb bigger losses.

CursorBench: Composer 2.5 scores 63.2% at $0.55/task, nearly matching Opus 4.7 Max and GPT 5.5 Extra High at 20x less cost ('insane value', 'the sleeper'); Gemini 3.5 Flash only #10 at 49.8% — 'the disappointment'.

macro.theme: AI hitting physical bottlenecks — compute scarcity
macro.view: Power and construction speed are the real limits; OpenAI's Stargate stalled completely (partner fights, no lender funding, grid power the #1 bottleneck), missed its 2025 compute target, now renting from Oracle/Microsoft/Google — and even Google is out of compute. Conclusion: 'He or she who has the most compute wins!'
'Textbook Jevons: Token price tanks, demand goes thru the roof' — collapsing AI token prices expand total demand rather than shrink revenue.
S&P 500 expected to grow profits +23% this year — 'That kind of profit growth has never happened before... the boom is being driven exclusively by AI.' Explains S&P at ATHs despite crummy macro.

macro.theme: Winners Take Most era / extreme wealth divide
macro.view: S&P +130% in 6 years while consumer sentiment fell -55% to the lowest since 1952. In the AGI age the gap goes parabolic: hard-asset holders 'lap everyone else at lightspeed'; potential for the first $100 TRILLION company over the next decade.

macro.theme: Consumer sentiment at record low — contrarian read
macro.view: Lowest UMich reading since 1952: a flashing warning for the economy but 'often a contrary (and sometimes bullish) signal for stocks. ie we are at rock bottom.'

macro.theme: Rates: cuts into a boom, AI is deflationary
macro.view: New Fed chair Kevin 'gets it' (unlike JP): AI will make almost everything cost less; 'probably in the early innings of a structural decline in prices'; central bank stuck on 1978 models might miss the productivity boom. Sounds like he will cut rates into a boom even though futures don't price it. 'PS I like Kevin. I did not like JP.'

Trump administration putting $2B into quantum companies for equity stakes (IBM $1B, GlobalFoundries $375M, D-Wave/Rigetti/Infleqtion ~$100M, Diraq $38M); quantum appears to be Trump's next major investment focus, and sectors he backs boom. MSM caught up a day later (INFQ +60% in 28 hours).

Daily active users across major chains fell 5.9M → 5.5M (-6.8%) from Jan to Apr 2026. Solana still the clear leader by a mile but cooling; Ethereum and smaller chains saw much steeper drops; 'Nice to see BTC holding up.'

Bankless co-founder David Hoffman — who shilled ETH for 6 years — sold all his personal ETH, now promoting ZEC and HYPE as 'survivors'. James's jab: 'They always hated SOLANA.' Reads as bearish-ETH, pro-SOL positioning signal.

Solana becoming the fast, cheap settlement layer for tokenized assets, stablecoins, AI agents (winning ~50-70% of agent activity) and consumer apps — ~$752B of structural SOL demand vs 578M circulating supply over ~5 years justifies a $1,000 target.

Saylor on CNBC: Strategy will buy BTC 'every quarter, forever', never sell, funded with 'digital credit' (STRC); 1M BTC left to mine and Strategy expects to buy it all — end game a 2M BTC bag. A permanent structural buyer under the market.
His EV math on FM Intelligence's Clarity Act scenarios: EV $117.5k, low $96.25k, high $138.75k vs ~$76.6k spot — even the conservative end shows meaningful upside; case for asymmetric long exposure.
Amplifies Adam Back's call: BTC is digital gold, hits gold parity this cycle, $1.5M by 2028 with a real wager behind it — 'If he is even half right I would be very impressed.'

SEC officially delayed its blockchain-based tokenized stock plan; BTC dropped ~$900 (-$15B mcap) on the news, which he finds irrational since Bitcoin is too slow to handle stock tokenization anyway — 'I remain confused.'

macro.theme: Fiat debasement and the millionaire explosion
macro.view: Millionaire share of population surging globally (US ~13% → ~18% of households in 9 years; Switzerland 28.65%, HK 24.88%, Singapore 22.83%). Primary driver is currency debasement, not productivity — 'the measuring stick (the currency) shrinking'. Conclusion: own hard assets.

Reads the tea leaves — Trump turning Air Force One around, Iranian IRGC-linked assassination plot against Ivanka, Pentagon pizza index at 'Doughcon Level 2', Iranian airspace empty — 'I reckon some type of weekend attack is loading.' Stocks down after hours.
Tesla officially confirmed FSD Supervised live in the US, Canada, Mexico, China, Australia, South Korea, Netherlands and more; stock moving up after hours — bullish robotaxi/FSD catalyst.

macro.theme: Tesla Optimus moat skepticism vs SpaceX Raptor moat
macro.view: Raptor V3's secret sauce (materials, internal cooling channels, manufacturing) can't be copied from the outside; Optimus's humanoid design reveals most of the engineering externally and Chinese manufacturers are excellent cloners — expect lookalike robots faster than people think. Implication: SpaceX's moat is more defensible than Tesla's robot moat.
Live-stream title only ('Liquidity Race Begins', May 22 1pm Pacific) — signals his focus on a new global liquidity expansion phase as a market driver.

AI infrastructure names consolidating after huge moves; Quantum and Space the two themes on fire; crypto miners ripping as they pivot to AI; S&P/Nasdaq grinding higher near records; BTC broke $76k then digesting.

frameworks.name: IA13
frameworks.description: His 13-name AI model portfolio/basket — the core engine of his equity book. TSLA is labeled "TSLA (IA13)" at 51.8% of the portfolio, with the other IA13/AI names (NVDA, AMD, MU, AVGO, ALAB, MRVL, GOOG, PLTR, EOSE, CPER, Echostar SpaceX proxy) at ~23%. He tracks its performance weekly ("IA13 fire continues", "strong IA13 performance" diluted his cash buffer percentage) and notes his big 3 IA13 gainers (ALAB, MRVL, MU) outperformed both his decade of BTC stacking and NVDA. Analyst upgrades on IA13 names are flagged as thesis validation.

frameworks.name: 3X investing (market-cap headroom test)
frameworks.description: Risk-reward test based on whether a 3x in market cap is mathematically plausible: "Will NVDA go to 16.5T? Probably not. Will TSLA go to 4.5T? Absolutely. This is the math and why TSLA remains the best risk reward." He sizes conviction by comparing current market cap to a believable future cap rather than by price action.

frameworks.name: Rule du Jour
frameworks.description: A numbered series of codified trading rules he publishes (this batch contains Rule du Jour #109: "Follow the Herd and you Lose"). Each rule distills a behavioral/contrarian principle into his decision process — here, avoiding consensus narratives and mainstream media in favor of pure uncorrupted data-driven decisions.

frameworks.name: Covered-call hedging (selling calls against stock, buying back cheap)
frameworks.description: He hedges long stock by selling calls against the position (his "Google Hedge": sold GOOG 6/12/2026 $385 calls for $15-16), then buys them back when premium decays or the thesis turns bullish again (covered at $9). The hedge is treated as a tactical income/protection overlay that he actively opens and closes around momentum shifts.

frameworks.name: Deep ITM LEAPS conversion
frameworks.description: He holds super deep in-the-money TSLA LEAPS ($140/$160/$175 strikes) and plans to convert them (exercise/roll into shares) in June, raising the required cash by selling other positions (STM) and exiting hedges. LEAPS provide leveraged exposure on his highest-conviction name, converted when deep ITM.

frameworks.name: IPO layering
frameworks.description: For big IPOs (SpaceX), he expects the post-IPO dump ("Everyone will dump the stock after IPO - some truth to this hence we need to time our layers correctly") and plans staged, layered entries rather than buying the open. Complemented by venue heuristics: Robinhood is the best avenue for pre-IPO allocations with sub-$100K accounts; SoFi allocations will be small.

frameworks.name: Only 3-5 Assets / 10-year test
frameworks.description: Concentration filter: if he could only own 3-5 assets for 10 years to capture the AI wave it would be TSLA, SPCX and NVDA, with the heaviest dose in TSLA — chosen for "limited downside and great upside over the next decade." He also believes TSLA/SPCX could disrupt NVDA with Terafab when the world goes to 99% inference, which informs the TSLA-heavy weighting.

frameworks.name: OCTA
frameworks.description: Named model referenced in his livestream "OCTA: Mu Moon Mission" — applied to project Micron's (MU) upside trajectory. (Only the title appears in this batch; the model itself is used to frame MU's "moon mission" move.)

frameworks.name: Bottoms-up valuation modeling (SpaceX)
frameworks.description: He builds his own multi-year valuation scenarios with explicit revenue assumptions (e.g., 2x EWS revenue → $2.8T by mid-2027, excluding optionality like Data Centers in Space, MacroHard, Terafab) and stress-tests them ("Even cut it in half and you still get a 6x"). The output directly drives buy decisions — he uses the math "to convince myself of buying a small slice of the IPO."

frameworks.name: Personal AI trading tutor (Claude Projects)
frameworks.description: Community method (via Jacob) he endorses: build a dedicated 24/7 trading tutor using Claude Projects loaded with the community's training PDFs, videos/transcripts and indicator guides, plus your own trade journals, chart screenshots, winning and failed setups — turning the AI into a customized reasoning engine for objective trade review and avoiding repeated mistakes.

rules.rule: Rule du Jour #109: Follow the herd and you lose. Original ideas that contradict the consensus are the only ideas that create real alpha — everything else is herd chasing that results in herd returns. Always go back to pure uncorrupted data-driven decisions.
rules.context: Stated amid "extraordinary negativity and outright derangement" in market narrative; he avoids mainstream media and most social platforms, keeping only a small curated X list

macro.view: S&P futures at new ATH; bears "will eventually be right but in the meantime enjoy the ride." SPX hit record 7,528; Ed Yardeni raised targets to 8,250 by end of 2026 and 10,000 by 2030; Tom Lee says the best stock market years in a generation are coming — today's prices are cheap if forecasts play out.

Computing power is power; data centers are the new factories deciding who leads in tech, medicine, defense, robotics, manufacturing. The real danger is the West blocking its own AI infrastructure build-out with bureaucracy while China/Russia just build — handing dominance to authoritarian regimes.

Remains bullish into the $1.75T IPO despite hater consensus: Starlink already profitable and growing, Starship will collapse launch costs, data centers in space "going to happen," huge Anthropic AI-infrastructure deal worth $15BN/year that bears don't factor in, far-and-away #1 in space. "THERE IS NOTHING BIGGER THAN SPACE." 5yr projection: $12T conservative, $25T bull by 2030/31.

Starcloud solved the hardest problem (satellite-to-satellite data movement via Starlink laser terminals), enabling AI inference (eventually training) in orbit with free solar power and vacuum cooling. SpaceX has filed plans for up to a million orbital computers. "The cloud is moving to space."

Space Force awarded SpaceX $2.29B for Starshield's Space Data Network backbone (secure military LEO comms, prototype by end-2027); NASA will lean heavily on SpaceX for the Moonbase with Starship doing all lifting and landing — deepening government revenue moat.

Bitcoin is more undervalued relative to global money supply than at any point in its history — but short-term bearish signals persist: ETFs selling off aggressively, negative press near ATH, price broke below the 50 DMA (above since April 5). "The question is when will it come back to life." Also tracking "Bitcoin Whales Vanishing" (livestream theme).

His focus has shifted to AI equities: "IA made more money in three months on three names that he did in 10 years stacking BTC" — ALAB, MRVL and MU smoked his decade-long BTC stack and leaped ahead of NVDA. Crypto is now the secondary bet (~26% of portfolio) behind AI (~75%).

Micron parabolic: UBS target $1,625, market cap broke $1T (up 1200% in a year), a $200+ single-day gain, "chart going vertical"; MRVL upgraded to $300 street-high on optical interconnect/data-center growth. He notes MU "causing a lot of the POMO" while warning vs herding.

Cybercab is the most efficient car ever made by a long shot — ~204 MPG-equivalent (1 L/100km); even Model 3 needs nearly a third more energy per mile. Also: CNBC reports Elon has discussed a full SpaceX-Tesla merger, and many SpaceX shareholders plan to rotate into TSLA once liquid post-IPO — both TSLA-bullish.

"AGI is already here." Marc Andreessen thinks the line was crossed 3 months ago with GPT-5.5, Claude 4.6, Gemini 3 and Grok 4.3 — nobody noticed because the field moves too fast to register milestones.

US at ~500,000 business applications per month (near record), but only ~1 in 3 ever hire employees — a boom in one-person operations (solo founders, freelancers, AI-enabled side hustles), not job-creating companies.

All of the world's richest (Musk $800B+, Google founders, Bezos, Zuckerberg, Jensen) built fortunes in technology. The fastest, most reliable way to create enormous wealth for founders AND investors is owning pieces of transformative tech companies that scale globally.

Hong Kong overtook Switzerland as the world's largest cross-border wealth hub ($2.9tn in 2025, ~60% from mainland China); Switzerland fell behind due to Western-Europe-oriented equity exposure with less in fast-growing markets.

MSTR used ~$1.4B of the cash reserved for 11.5% STRC preferred dividends to buy back $1.5B of its 0% debt at an 8% discount — cleans the balance sheet and helps S&P 500 inclusion odds, but spends most of the dividend safety buffer; if capital raising gets hard they may have to sell Bitcoin or dilute. A bet they can refill cash while BTC rises. (He holds 12.5% MSTR + STRC as his cash buffer.)

frameworks.name: IA13
frameworks.description: His curated list of 13 highest-conviction names (started ~June 1, 2025, exactly one year before these emails). Anduril has been in it since day 1. He tracks which IA13 names have 'popped' vs not — on May 29 he noted PLTR and TSLA were the only IA13 names that hadn't popped yet, with the expectation that 'the cream always floats to the top'. The list anchors what he buys, holds and writes about.

frameworks.name: Mean Reversion (MR) signal
frameworks.description: A quantified oscillator-style signal (readings like +3 elevated, -2 oversold) used on multiple timeframes to time exits and entries. PANW exit: 'MEAN REVERSION WAS SCREAMING SELL'. PLTR at +3 = near-term pullback expected. MSTR 4-hour MR at -2 near support. NVDA: a mean-reversion move lower would be a 'great place to add'. BTC: bullish divergence = price making lower lows while mean reversion rises.

frameworks.name: Confluence model
frameworks.description: A multi-indicator chart model with bands, trend colors (orange = downtrend/caution, flip to blue = safer entry) and 'shadows'. PANW showed a 'SELL SHADOW' confirming his exit; BTC's 4-hour confluence model showed a buy signal with bullish divergence; ARM was 'well outside the bands' meaning too extended to buy. He uses confluence + mean reversion together for buy/sell timing.

frameworks.name: ATR levels / tiered buy zones (tier 4, tier 5)
frameworks.description: A ladder of pullback buy zones derived from ATR ('ATR level 5' etc.). Deeper tiers = better adds: MU's ATR level 5 sits at ~750, ARM's at ~270; for AMD he'd only buy a pullback 'into tier 4 or 5'. Used to avoid chasing extended names and pre-define where to stack.

frameworks.name: The Weekly Edge / #TAlpha
frameworks.description: A weekly technical-analysis summary (built from Jacob's video) covering indices, every key IA13/semis/crypto name with trend state, mean-reversion readings, ATR-tier buy zones, and 'Key Trades Noted'. It's the weekly playbook: this edition's verdict was 'Everything Extended' — trim, take profits, raise cash.

frameworks.name: Trading BOX
frameworks.description: Range-trading framework: define a box and buy the bottom / sell the top until breakout. Example he ran for 6 weeks on PLTR: buy at $130, sell near $156-160 — which nearly filled two days after the latest buy.

frameworks.name: Synthetic Longs (SL) converting to stock
frameworks.description: He builds positions via synthetic longs (options structures); when exercised/converted they become stock positions. PANW was 'a SL that converted to stock' held over a year before he sold the converted shares; KEEL was similarly 'a small position that converted from Options'. Once converted and small/extended, he exits the equity.

frameworks.name: LEAPS dip-buying + 'Margin Mattress'
frameworks.description: Core wealth-compounding engine: buy deep dips in conviction names with long-dated (often OTM) LEAPS instead of stock for leveraged delta (e.g., TSLA $140-strike LEAPS bought when stock was $106; March 2020 LEAPS at $17). Convert LEAPS to stock at expiry. The exploded equity value becomes his 'Margin Mattress' — collateral used to buy other things and sell puts.

frameworks.name: Covered calls / theta harvesting
frameworks.description: Sells covered calls on large holdings and buys them back cheaper as time decay works: GOOGL 385 calls sold at $15, bought back at $9 for $6-7 net while the stock went nowhere — 'the power of theta even when price goes nowhere'.

frameworks.name: Rule du Jour (numbered rules series)
frameworks.description: Recurring numbered-rule emails distilling his principles. Rule #110 'Minting Wealth': being early + conviction + patience beats almost everything else; the later you arrive, the smaller your slice of the pie — illustrated with SpaceX employee equity (17,000x for 2008 joiners vs $1M for post-2016 joiners).

frameworks.name: Layering (LAYER IN / LAYER OUT)
frameworks.description: Position entry/exit discipline: never go 'big bang' into anything, especially IPOs — scale in and out in layers because new issues can dip in the subsequent 90 days. Applied to the SpaceX IPO cash-allocation planning.

frameworks.name: Data-driven valuation ratios for crypto (mcap/DAU, mcap/transaction)
frameworks.description: 'I only invest when DATA DRIVEN DECISIONS support my thesis.' For L1 comparisons he uses market cap per daily active user and market cap per transaction — on those, SOL is far cheaper than SUI, so he refuses to buy SUI despite the dip and the outage.

rules.rule: Rule #110 (Minting Wealth): Being early + conviction + patience beats almost everything else in investing. Get in early on transformative companies and HOLD winners through volatility — the later you arrive, the smaller your slice of the pie. Most people do the opposite: buy late when it's 'safe' and sell at the first big dip.
rules.context: Illustrated with SpaceX: 2008-era employees who held saw up to 17,000x; 400-500 people worth $100M+; same engineers doing identical work are worth 100x different amounts purely on start date + holding period.

rules.rule: Do everything in LAYERS — LAYER IN, LAYER OUT. Do NOT go big bang on an IPO, as it could dip in the subsequent 90 days.
rules.context: Guidance ahead of the SpaceX IPO; also notes IPO shares are non-marginable at Schwab/Fidelity — you need 100% settled cash to cover allocated shares, margin cannot be used.

rules.rule: 1 trade, maybe 2 per week is optimal for most people. Overtrading leads to losing money over time — fees, taxes, emotional mistakes, getting whipsawed by noise. Lower-frequency, patient trading beats hyperactive trading for the vast majority of retail investors.
rules.context: Reacting to data showing Interactive Brokers customers trade ~4x more per account than Robinhood/Fidelity/Schwab clients. 'Slow and steady wins.'

rules.rule: Sell when price reaches the cluster of raised analyst targets AND mean reversion is screaming sell AND the confluence model shows a sell shadow — take the exit even on a year-old conviction position.
rules.context: The PANW exit at $273.51 after Benchmark ($270), Wedbush ($300) and BTIG (~$268) target raises were essentially hit pre-earnings.

rules.rule: Clear out dust positions: if a holding is a tiny fraction of the portfolio (e.g. 0.00375) and 20x smaller than your core exposure to the same theme, it's noise — sell it even if you think it goes higher, and consolidate.
rules.context: RIOT sale: 'too nervous with too many holdings'; Micron position is 20x larger, so miners exposure was redundant.

rules.rule: When a small options-converted equity position is sitting near all-time highs, take the win and leave — 'Chart was near ATH... time to go,' even acknowledging 'it will prob go higher'.
rules.context: KEEL (ex-Bitfarms) exit at $5.42.

rules.rule: Only invest when DATA DRIVEN DECISIONS support the thesis — don't buy a dip just because it's down or because famous investors are in it.
rules.context: Refusing to buy SUI after its outage/dump because SOL is far cheaper on market cap per DAU and per transaction; 'I feel bad for Raul Pal, who went all in on Sui.'

rules.rule: Do not fade Elon — 'he unthinks stuff and finds a better way.'
rules.context: On SpaceX building a custom C-based training stack claimed to be 10x faster than Google's JAX for its 220k GB300 cluster.

rules.rule: 'Eventually the cream always floats to the top' — quality IA13 names that lag will pop; be positioned before the catalyst, don't chase after.
rules.context: PLTR was the only IA13 name along with TSLA that hadn't popped; he'd been waiting 4-6 weeks, holding the box trade, and it jumped ~8% on drone-funding news.

rules.rule: When everything is extended (9th straight green week, semis up 27-81% in 30 days), trim, take profits and raise cash — don't chase; wait for pullbacks into ATR tier 4/5 buy zones instead.
rules.context: The 'Everything Extended!!!' Weekly Edge; he matched the words with action by selling PANW, RIOT and KEEL the same week. Focus on buying dips rather than predicting exact tops.

rules.rule: Buy dips and use LEAPS in conviction names — the leverage on deep dips (TSLA $106 → $140-strike LEAPS) creates the life-changing returns the stock alone never gives; then convert at expiry and recycle the equity ('Margin Mattress') to buy other things and sell puts.
rules.context: Pushing back on 'Tesla has not moved in 5 years' FUD: stock +106% in 5 years, but the LEAPS-on-dips approach made dramatically more.

rules.rule: Beware extreme retail options crowding — when retail call-buying hits records, market makers (Citadel) hedge by buying stock, amplifying upside, but the same hedging accelerates the drop on a reversal. They see your flow in real time and 'may try make a play against us.'
rules.context: Record retail options volume on semiconductors, ~3x normal. 'Ken has made $50BN trading against us.'

rules.rule: Follow the money behind every narrative — check whether the person pushing a story has bags that benefit from it before acting on it.

macro.view: Rejects the railroad analogy (BofA chart): railroads/dot-com telecom had hard ceilings, while 'AI faces what looks like infinite demand for intelligence' that raises productivity in every industry and improves itself. But the 40% concentration reading is flashing and 'there will be casualties along the way.' Ackman agrees: markets going way higher, no bubble, many names actually cheap given the AI revolution.

Retail is crushing professional fund managers: retail-favorite big tech/AI/chip stocks beat mutual-fund favorites by 16 percentage points in May — biggest gap since tracking began in 2018 — and retail is spending ~5x the normal monthly amount on AI stock options (all-time high).

Massively bullish per big-money insiders despite huge retail bearish sentiment: $1.8T target valuation, $80B raise, 12x oversubscribed, lockups mostly >366 days (limited selling pressure), $28.5T TAM across 20+ business lines (asteroid mining 'very real'), EWS coherent compute 'mispriced' and could eclipse Anthropic in a year (>$1T alone). Insiders see $2.3T within days of IPO; BlackRock-style players plan to buy big at IPO and rotate into index flows on S&P 500/Nasdaq-100 inclusion.
Anthropic confidentially filed an S-1 and aims to IPO between September and November 2026 — possibly going public before OpenAI.

The whole industry is compute supply-constrained through 2027 ('if folks say they have compute capacity for 2027 they are full of it' — OpenAI CFO). Bottlenecks: electricity, server racks, chips, memory, talent. 1GW of power costs ~$50B to set up but may yield only ~$10B revenue — the numbers don't add up, hence renting compute (Oracle, CoreWeave). Sora paused for lack of compute. OpenAI raised $130B (largest ever), >900M weekly ChatGPT users, ads coming ('If Google and Meta had a child, it would be ChatGPT'). James would NOT buy the OpenAI IPO; thinks the humanoid-robot claim is BS.

AI agents are the next computing wave and this time DRAM-per-user shoots almost straight up: ~5x more memory per person in 5 years. Clear beneficiaries he names: Micron, hyperscalers including SpaceX, TSMC, NVIDIA, AMD, AVGO.

Analytical SaaS is dead; infrastructure SaaS (MongoDB, Databricks, Oracle) undervalued with enterprises needing 10x more data storage in 3 years; system-of-record companies (Salesforce/CRM, HR) will be rewired or killed as agents replace interfaces ('the era of the interface is dead'). Frontier models have little/no moat — weights fit on a USB stick, users not loyal (a view James says he has long warned about). Autonomy is a $10T opportunity (aka Tesla; Waymo loses money scaling). Security more important than ever.

Coatue's new Magnificent 8 private companies: SpaceX, Anduril, Stripe, Anthropic, Databricks, OpenAI, Revolut, ByteDance. Two are already in his IA13 (SpaceX, Anduril). He wants exposure to Databricks: 'DATA DATA DATA.... WE NEED TO GET SOME DATABRICKS.'

Copper is the physical bottleneck of AI + electrification + onshoring: parallel $1T+ buildouts (aerospace, grid, power gen, data centers/semis, defense rising from 3.3% to 5% of GDP by 2030) vs catastrophic supply constraints (7–12 yrs per mine, ~0 new major mines before 2030, ore grades -40% since 1991; world needs 100 new mines by 2045). Price to double to $13/lb.

Secondary markets are blowing up — private shares now sell at a premium to NAV ('a game changer'); $250M/$1B/$5B pre-IPO deals; billions traded in SpaceX, Anthropic, Anduril. Forge Global (he met the CEO) has 'amazing plans incoming'; regulated SPVs via Schwab + Forge make safe retail access possible — but most SPVs are cowboys (Anthropic and OpenAI want theirs dissolved).

BTC rejected off the 200 DMA and 'down only' since; back under his $64K entry. Nobody at the Liquidity Summit talked BTC/crypto — all AI, SpaceX, some energy. Saylor not liked or trusted among the smart money; crypto morale at all-time low (Hoskinson says ADA is dead); ETFs dumping; rumor the crypto group is selling to YOLO into AI and SpaceX. He is not buying any crypto and is harvesting cash.

Cybercabs arrived in Austin 'en masse'; FSD added an 11th country — 'slowly then suddenly.' At $99/month per car this is billions in margin per year. Separately, autonomy framed as a $10T market (Arora), with Tesla the implied winner over Waymo.

Alphabet raising $80B equity specifically for AI infrastructure (incl. $10B Berkshire private placement) — 'very strong signal' a top hyperscaler is doubling down. AVGO is a key beneficiary via the multi-year custom-TPU deal through 2031 plus networking content.

Starlink is not just competing in the $400B global cell market — it's expanding the connectivity TAM (remote areas, AI devices, autonomous machines) and positioned to capture or create trillions in value; a category-defining play, not a niche satellite business.

'We're probably moving past the picks and shovels stage into whatever comes next' — attending the Liquidity Summit to learn where the smartest money sees the next 5 years and to get early access to private deals like Anduril. David Sacks: $8 trillion of wealth in the room.

Strategy sold 32 BTC at avg $77,135 (holdings down to 843,706 BTC) to fund preferred distributions — 'So much for never selling,' though he concedes $77K was a good sale price vs ~$71K spot. The sale shook the STRC market.

frameworks.name: Rule du Jour (numbered rules series)
frameworks.description: A numbered, codified rulebook he publishes one rule at a time (this batch contains Rule #111). Each rule pairs a principle with a live trade example from his own book — he uses them as guardrails and post-mortems on his own decisions.

frameworks.name: Retire On Cocktail
frameworks.description: His named model portfolio / retirement compounding plan, updated annually via video — the 2026 update targets turning $280K into $7M by 2032. Used as the long-horizon allocation blueprint for followers.

frameworks.name: Rotation as core portfolio discipline
frameworks.description: Tracks where capital is rotating (e.g., into the AI complex starting Nov 2022 after ChatGPT) and positions years before retail. Uses the pattern 'retail capitulates at the bottom and rotates into the hot area at the top years too late' to fade late-cycle crowd moves.

frameworks.name: 200 WMA + ATR buy signal (BTC)
frameworks.description: BTC's 200-week moving average ($62K) is his historically reliable bear-market support with 'very good risk reward' — but he requires a confirming ATR-based buy signal before deploying. At the level twice in 2026 with no signal, he watches and does not buy.

frameworks.name: Coins-in-loss capitulation metric
frameworks.description: On-chain bottom-detection gauge: number of BTC held at a loss vs historical capitulation extremes (10.7M in 2018, 10.4M Covid, 10.6M in 2022). At 10.2M coins in the red he concludes 'the bottom is real close.'

frameworks.name: Crypto fundamentals key metric (revenue/activity)
frameworks.description: Judges chains by the fundamental metric 'that matters in the traditional world' (revenue / activity on human attention) rather than narrative. By this metric SOL 'is eating everyone's lunch' while other chains are 'expensive decorations on the blockchain.'

frameworks.name: MAG8 Elite Tier
frameworks.description: His tiering of the AI era's ultimate winners — 'the winners that will take most and also pull in 80% of all funding.' Used to filter which names are must-own; Databricks is positioned in it as a foundational picks-and-shovels data play.

frameworks.name: SaaS 4-parts framework
frameworks.description: Decomposes SaaS into 4 parts, of which 2 are dead; the surviving, critical part is SAAS INFRA (including data). Drives his thesis that data infrastructure (10x more data stored within 3 years) makes Databricks massively undervalued.

frameworks.name: Synthetic Longs + LEAPs financed by short puts/calls
frameworks.description: His core options structure (shown on ALAB): LEAP calls / synthetic longs as the main bullish position, short puts sold to finance the LEAPs, and covered calls sold against half the position for premium. Managed dynamically — buys back short calls on dips, covers short puts to free margin, and trims LEAPs if short calls get run over.

frameworks.name: IA AI Names forward-PE screen
frameworks.description: A maintained table of his AI universe with checkmarks on names he owns, screened on forward P/E to find mispricing — e.g., SNDK, MU, NVDA 'stunningly low' vs INTC and ARM 'looking rich.'

frameworks.name: Liquidity Alpha (smart-money intel series)
frameworks.description: Distills what top VCs/allocators at the 'Liquidity Event' are buying (Hot Names: Neura, Zipline, Databricks; consensus trade 'GO LONG ELON'). He treats allocator consensus vs X/retail doom as a signal — but warns 'VCs often TALK THEIR OWN BOOK so beware' and filters each name through his own buy/no-buy judgment.

frameworks.name: Multi-broker IPO allocation ('hooks in all ponds')
frameworks.description: For hot IPOs (SpaceX): place simultaneous orders on every retail venue (Robinhood, Fidelity, Schwab) plus private connections, sized at ~80% of buying power, knowing fills will be heavily prorated — maximizing total allocation at the offering price.

rules.rule: Rule #111: 'Never Jump in Front of a Freight Train' — never let cash pressure force you into trades you wouldn't normally make; raising cash in a panic will cost you far more in the long run.
rules.context: Stated after getting caught offside selling calls on ALAB while desperate to raise cash, forcing him to sell ~12% of his LEAP position.

rules.rule: The danger of covered calls: sometimes the stock takes off and you get stuck on the wrong side of the trade ('caught offside') — selling calls caps your upside even when you own protective LEAPs.
rules.context: ALAB kept climbing past his short call strikes; per 100 LEAP calls he had to sell ~12 to cover the short calls.

rules.rule: VCs often TALK THEIR OWN BOOK — so beware when top investors pitch their favorite names.

rules.rule: Retail investors capitulate at the bottom and rotate into the new hot area at the top, years too late — don't be that flow; rotation is a core portfolio discipline.

If EWS pressures AWS/GCP/Azure pricing, hyperscaler ROI gets worse — only AMZN currently looks positive-ROI on AI capex. SpaceX spent ~$20B on AI capex and recoups $15B in 12 months.

frameworks.name: LEAPS leverage / synthetic longs
frameworks.description: Core position-building tool: 'IA utilizes leaps to get leverage and build a winning position without paying full price.' Implementation shown in the TSLA trade — buy deep-dated calls and simultaneously sell puts (380C bought / 390P sold, 924 DTE, $40 net debit) to create a synthetic long with a defined breakeven ($420). Also prescribes setups like the PLTR $120-strike 18-month LEAP when stock falls below $130.

frameworks.name: Covered calls as income + mean-reversion buybacks
frameworks.description: Sells covered calls on holdings (e.g. ALAB) and buys them back when the underlying is due for mean reversion or dips — 'bought back a chunk of his covered calls... covered the remainder today on the dip.' Pairs with hedges (GOOGL hedge) as active risk management. He criticizes hedge funds for using 'no risk mgt tools whatsoever, no options, no hedges, no covered calls.'

frameworks.name: Sniper / buy-zone deployment
frameworks.description: During corrections, pre-define key buy zones per asset (NVDA sub-200, MU 650-675, AVGO 350-378, TSM 340, SOL 45, etc.) and wait for the market to come to you: 'act like snipers in our key buy zones... Keep your powder dry, let the dust settle... deploy your capital surgically.' Uses 50-day/200-day moving averages and sideways consolidation as confirmation; explicitly anti-chasing and anti-falling-knife.

frameworks.name: Rule du Jour (numbered rules series)
frameworks.description: A running series of numbered investing rules. Rule #112 'the Hedge Fund Scam': never hand capital to high-fee, long-lockup vehicles (2-2.5% mgmt + 20% profit share + 3-5yr lockups). Models that 2/20 fees turn $100K at 7% over 40 years from ~$1,497,446 into ~$480,102 — over $1M extracted in fees. Conclusion: own high-conviction assets directly in public markets, keep liquidity, sell calls on top. 'BE A COMPOUNDER.'

frameworks.name: TABI model (power-law quantile)
frameworks.description: A power-law quantile valuation model for Bitcoin. James uses where price sits in the model's historical quantile distribution to call value zones: on June 11 BTC was in the bottom ~4% of all modeled history, and only 70 of the last 2,535 days (~2.7%) were this deep 'blue' — his signal that 'Bitcoin is cheap as chips' and a strong accumulation zone.

frameworks.name: MR (Mean Reversion) signal
frameworks.description: An oversold/overbought mean-reversion indicator on individual names. When TSLA was 'deep oversold on the MR' it triggered his June 10 add — MR oversold readings function as buy triggers.

frameworks.name: Confluence (multi-signal buy confirmation)
frameworks.description: He waits for multiple independent buy signals to line up ('I am getting multiple buy signals on Confluence') before pulling the trigger; combined with MR oversold, this confirmed the TSLA synthetic-long add at $384.

frameworks.name: Synthetic long (LEAP + short put)
frameworks.description: Replicates stock exposure cheaply: buy a deep LEAP call and sell a put at a nearby strike for a small net debit (TSLA: bought $380 LEAP 2028, sold $390 put 2028, net debit $34, breakeven $414 over 919 days). Management rule: when the short put goes deep out of the money, buy it back for pennies. Payoff framing: 'throw down $30 to make $270.'

frameworks.name: IA13
frameworks.description: His curated 13-name AI stock portfolio/list (TSLA, NVDA, AMD, MU, AVGO, ALAB, MRVL, GOOG, PLTR etc). He tracks its performance against BTC (strong IA13 performance diluted his STRC cash buffer from 11% to 8.8%) and uses it as the vehicle for the AI supercycle thesis; plan for names like MRVL is hold to 2028.

frameworks.name: Retire On bag / Retire On Cocktail
frameworks.description: A concentrated 'set it and forget it' position sized to retire on, built at a key entry (e.g., 655 SPCX shares for $88K at the $135 IPO). The 'Retire on Cocktail' has an average CAGR of nearly 100% if you exit at all-time highs. Used for asymmetric long-term compounding plays like 'Retire On SpaceX.'

frameworks.name: OCTA
frameworks.description: His on-chain/technical analysis framework for Bitcoin market structure (referenced in the 'OCTA: Historic Shakeout' video covering a 'crazy new bottom signal,' the ARMA 20-yr lockup, AI fire and scarcity update). Used to identify historic shakeouts and bottom conditions in BTC.

frameworks.name: Supply-in-loss bottom indicator
frameworks.description: On-chain bottom-finder: when BTC supply sitting at a loss peaks and converges with supply in profit (10.5M BTC at a loss = peak bear levels), it has always marked cycle bottoms. He marks the peaks with red arrows and treats it as a contrarian accumulation signal.

frameworks.name: Covered-call hedging with intentional assignment
frameworks.description: He hedges single names by selling covered calls (e.g., on STM and ALAB), then either covers the hedge (ALAB) or deliberately lets shares get called away (STM) when he wants to raise cash — assignment doubles as a cash-raising exit.

frameworks.name: Deep-ITM LEAPS conversion
frameworks.description: When LEAPS go super deep in the money, he converts them (into stock), funding the conversion with cash raised elsewhere (e.g., STM assignment proceeds earmarked for converting deep-ITM Tesla LEAPS in June).

frameworks.name: Tech IPO base rates
frameworks.description: Uses historical tech IPO return statistics (57% positive; +4% 1mo, +20% 3mo, +14% 1yr; standouts PLTR/ARM/DDOG/ZM/MDB) to set expectations and rebut fear narratives like '50% drawdown on SPCX IPO' — while noting SpaceX is unprecedented in size and IPO rules.

macro.view: The SPCX IPO ($135) is the event of the cycle: Polymarket prices a $2.17T closing cap (23% day-1 pop); confirmed at least 5x oversubscribed with long-only funds getting preferred status; BlackRock's Larry Fink arranging a $5bn+ slug and launching a Space ETF; people who sold assets to raise IPO cash will have to buy them back. First analyst PTs $190 (Oppenheimer) and $165 (New Street). He sees SpaceX as 'the ultimate $18T AI & Space catalyst' and cheap at $1.75T.

Bullish on the Musk ecosystem (Tesla + SpaceX + xAI): Terafab is a 100M sq ft chip factory (10x Giga Texas) targeting 1 TW of chips per year (~2x the entire US grid's average power), feeding hundreds of GW of chips for Optimus robots and terawatts for solar-powered AI satellites in orbit; millions of tons launched yearly via Starship and up to 1 billion Optimus robots. 'Nobody has ever even attempted a fraction of this scale... it is WILD.'

Denmark approved FSD — 'Slowly then Suddenly': millions of cars at $100/month is billions of pure profit. Piper Sandler says Tesla has 'solved the self-driving puzzle' and effectively achieved Level 4 (PT $500); unsupervised robotaxis growing daily. TSLA will pop in the latter part of 2026; the Tesla/SpaceX merger is NOT happening in 2026.

Jensen Huang says the AI build-out is speeding up — H2 much bigger than H1, next year huge. Bullish NVDA and MU (every Nvidia chip needs HBM memory). Claude 5 Fable shows capability still leaping (migrated a 50M-line Ruby codebase at Stripe in one day vs 6 months for a team) — AI progress is not plateauing.

Deepening US-China fight over chips/AI: Taiwan tightening export rules toward US policy; China spending ~$295B on data centers with 80% domestic hardware to push NVDA/AMD out; Pentagon added BABA, BIDU, BYD to its Chinese military-company list — bearish, triggers US fund selling and delisting risk. He holds BABA but is monitoring carefully.

Multiple bottom signals converging: 10.5M BTC in loss (peak bear levels that have always marked bottoms), BTC in the cheapest ~4% of its modeled history on the TABI/power-law quantile model (only ~2.7% of the last 2,535 days this cheap). Risk/reward 'phenomenal'; would add big at $50k. But diminishing returns may cap the next top at $150k unless the scarcity narrative ignites.

macro.theme: Scarcity in the age of AGI / AI wealth rotating into BTC
macro.view: 'AI agents will not fuel bitcoin, but wealth from AI will' — after monster AI returns, sophisticated investors rotate into scarce store-of-value assets; trillions unlocked by AI/tech IPOs could flow into BTC if it stays attractively valued. MSTR's CEO jumped on the narrative, but James thinks Saylor blaming BTC's price on AI is '3 years too late.'

Inflation rose from 2.4% (Feb) to 4.2% in 3 months, ~60% oil-driven, spooking markets; talk has shifted to Fed hikes. His view: the Fed does not control inflation, it only destroys demand — AI deflation could not come at a better time.

AI has been the faster horse for years; he's focused on 'easy AI money' (IA13) while keeping crypto as the secondary bet, ready to jump in on a big crypto tank. Crypto sentiment at conferences is poor — 'Saylor is not liked and truly remains in the wild west.' Metaplanet chart 'looks dead'; Metaplanet vs MRVL is 'straight vertical' (in MRVL's favor).

SPCX entered a $920M/month cloud agreement with GOOGL through 2029 for compute capacity — evidence of the AI/compute intensity behind Starlink+AI revenue projections ($195B sales, $65B operating profit by 2030 per analysts).
